author: @LoganKearsley
text: A good way to think about why this works is to consider each diameter of a gear as a lever. With a fixed axis, a gear operates as a class-1 lever, and if it's a concentric gear, the two arms are of the same length, so moving one end will move the other end the same distance in the opposite direction. If you allow the axis to move, though, then the axis is no longer the fulcrum--it will be either the load or the input, converting the gear into a class-2 or class-3 lever. If the gear is concentric, the non-fulcrum end of the diameter is twice as far from the fulcrum as the axis, so you obviously get a mechanical advantage of 2:1 or 1:2.

author: @jamesross1003
text: You know, after mulling this over for a bit it has occurred to me that this could literally be taken as a 3-body problem.  Well, I really mean the mechanism in the linkage of the two when put together.  Intuition would be that it should be 4 to 1 ratio, but it is 3 to 1.  On the surface of the problem it appears as it should be the 4-1 but is in fact 3-1 when you dig into it.  Much more of a head scratcher than appears to be sure.

author: @Ron-yj4rh
text: Again, another path to a CVT. A transmission can be made from differentials. Imagine a primary engine driving. And two electric motors turning the differential. One at 3:1, the other 2:1. In reality, you would have a three speed transmission BUT the transition would be seemless because the differential would make up for the difference in the drive and output speed. The differential could have a clutch pack in the middle to engage at a specific speed.

author: @billcrane2444
text: I believe the reason this gets a 2:1 reduction is because the contact area of the spline gears to the common gear are across the entire gear radius which averages at the half-way point, thus 1/2 the turning radius equaling a 2:1 ratio.

author: @tonybrock5288
text: This relates to that classic SAT Test in the US (Scholastic Assessment Test) that many (including the examiners!) get wrong. ie: If you rotate circle "a" around circle "b", how many revolutions? One assumes, in the case of equal sized wheels/circles that the circumference is the same and therefore only one revolution. This is correct if both are in fixed positions and are rotating equally against each other. However, if one wheel (gear) is static, as in your example, then the other wheel has to do all the travelling and rotating around this fixed point for both gears, and so, the rotating wheel has to do the job of both of them, whilst the other does none.  So it will need to do 2 revolutions not one. So the ratio is 2:1. And so on, for other ratios...

author: @WarblesWarkly
text:
I know that anyone who is interested:

Yes, you can get wildly strong gears 3D printing them. For certain projects I have personally used nylon (PA-6) and glass filled, and carbon fiber filled (PA-6 G/CF).

While the PA-6 glass and carbon both have a textured surface it's not all bad. A light air sanding and a few coats of cerakote and they are surprisingly rugged.

Yes, you can bake the PA-6 blends at 175° C (350° F). As long as you vacuum dry your parts they'll be fine. Vacuum drying for cerakote takes 5-7 days under the fullest vacuum you can. Longer if you live close to water (lake, river, ocean, etc).

author: @Marian_Martin_Morgenstern
text:
Yea-no, it still is behaving like normal gears, you just split the larger one into two of equal size
You are not rotating three gears one after the other but driving one gear over a stationary one, not unlike a planetary gear when you drive the planet gears over a stationary Sun gear.
Those circumferences add up and its always just the way one gear has traveled by how much it moved the other

I hope this is understandable, but English isn't my main language and I'm more fluent in contract and sales speak then Tech(besides IT and Networking gear)
